title: How Marty Supreme lost its grip on the Oscar contest
text: How Marty Supreme Lost Oscar Momentum. Based on the article, the film faced five major challenges: 1. Director Controversy: Allegations emerged in late January regarding Josh Safdie's conduct on previous film sets, creating negative publicity just days after Oscar nominations were announced. 2. Confusing Marketing Strategy: The campaign included unconventional promotional tactics like an orange blimp and an 18-minute meta-marketing video that may all prove to be too awkward for older-skewing Oscar voters to forget, particularly the Schwap! catchphrase. 3. Overconfident Messaging: Chalamet's declaration that his performance was probably my best and represented top-level sh*t came across as arrogant to some voters, though he later adopted a more humble approach. 4. Controversial Co-Star: Kevin O'Leary's visible alignment with the Trump administration in late January alienated liberal Academy voters who comprise much of the electorate. 5. Precursor Award Shutouts: The film experienced a historic 0-for-11 loss at the BAFTA Awards and failed to win at recent guild ceremonies, losing crucial momentum. As the article notes, the thing that matters most at the Oscars is momentum. Michael B. Jordan's Sinners subsequently became the frontrunner for Best Actor.
